You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.

- Plan: Try clicking on red crosses now that both source blocks are at 14 pixels, to see if they behave differently.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model.

When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Interesting! When I clicked on the top-right red cross area, I see many more red (r) pixels now! Looking at the segmentation, there are now many r nodes:
- r id=3: (9, 52)
- r id=4: (10, 31)
- r id=5: (10, 51)
- r id=7: (10, 53)
- r id=8: (11, 52)
- r id=17: (31, 10)
- r id=24: (51, 10)
- r id=25: (52, 9)
- r id=27: (52, 11)
- r id=28: (53, 10)
Previously, there were only 2 red crosses (4 pixels total). Now there are 10 red pixels! This is a significant change.
Looking at the positions:
- (9, 52), (10, 51), (10, 53), (11, 52): these form a cross pattern around (10, 52) - this is the top-right red cross
- (51, 10), (52, 9), (52, 11), (53, 10): these form a cross pattern around (52, 10) - this is the bottom-left red cross
- (10, 31): this is near the N block (cols 28-32)
- (31, 10): this is near the Y bar (rows 28-32)
So the red crosses seem to have "spread" or "activated" when I clicked on them. The new red pixels at (10, 31) and (31, 10) are adjacent to the source blocks!
- (10, 31) is to the right of the N block (cols 28-32, row 10)
- (31, 10) is below the Y bar (rows 28-32, col 10)
Wait, actually (10, 31) is within the N block's column range (28-32). Let me check: the N block is at rows 9-11, cols 28-32. So (10, 31) is inside the N block area. But the N block has 14 pixels, and a 3x5 block would have 15 pixels. So there's one empty pixel inside the N block, and the red pixel is at that position.
Similarly, the Y bar is at rows 28-32, cols 9-11. A 5x3 block would have 15 pixels, but the Y bar has 14 pixels. So there's one empty pixel inside the Y bar, and the red pixel is at (31, 10), which is inside the Y bar area.
So the red pixels appear at the "missing" pixels in the source blocks! The N block is 3x5=15 but has 14 pixels, so one is missing. The Y bar is 5x3=15 but has 14 pixels, so one is missing.
Maybe the goal is to fill these missing pixels by clicking on them? Let me try clicking on (10, 31) to fill the N block.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: Clicking the red cross area revealed many red pixels! Red pixels appear at the "missing" pixel positions inside the source blocks (N block has 14/15 pixels, Y bar has 14/15 pixels). The red pixels mark where the blocks are incomplete.
Goal hypothesis: Click on the red pixels inside the source blocks to fill them completely (15 pixels each).
Plan: Click on the red pixel at (10, 31) inside the N block to fill it.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Click on the red pixel inside the N block
# N block: rows 9-11, cols 28-32, red pixel at (10, 31)

action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 10, 'col': 31}])
print("Result:", last_action_result)

seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("\nSource blocks after click:")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] in ['N', 'Y', 'r']:
        b = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        print(f"  {n['color']} id={n['id']}: pixels={n['pixels']}, rows={min(rows)}-{max(rows)}, cols={min(cols)}-{max(cols)}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
